1130 you need a real integer, pipe it through `int`: 1131 1132 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1133 1134 {{ 42.55|round|int }} 1135 -> 43 1136 """ 1137 if method not in {"common", "ceil", "floor"}: 1138 raise FilterArgumentError("method must be common, ceil or floor") 1139 1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum( 1235 environment: "Environment", 1236 iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", 1237 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1238 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1239 ) -> V:

"""Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's
:func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for
+    You can also pass ``with_counts=True`` to include the number of
+    items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples
+    instead, without needing to materialize the group again with
+        <ul>{% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
The attribute supports dot notation for nested access.
+        for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr):
for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr)
+        for key, values in groupby(sorted_value, expr):

content.text: Now let's verify it works and check for peer messages.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [{'city': 'NY', 'name': 'a'}, {'city': 'NY', 'name': 'b'}, {'city': 'LA', 'name': 'c'}]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Works correctly and backward compatibility is preserved.

diff --git a/src/jinja2/filters.py b/src/jinja2/filters.py index 80ea6504..977f5809 100644 --- a/src/jinja2/filters.py +++ b/src/jinja2/filters.py @@ -1157,12 +1157,27 @@ class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): return tuple.__str__(self) +class _GroupTupleWithCount(t.NamedTuple): + grouper: t.Any + list: t.List + count: int + + # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print + # out the value during debugging. + def __repr__(self) -> str: + return tuple.__repr__(self) + + def __str__(self) -> str: + return tuple.__str__(self) + + @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for @@ -1203,6 +1218,20 @@ def sync_do_groupby(
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %} + You can also pass ``with_counts=True`` to include the number of + items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples + instead, without needing to materialize the group again with + ``|list|length``. + + .. sourcecode:: jinja + +
      {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %} +
    • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
      • + {% endfor %}
    + + .. versionchanged:: 3.1 + Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. + .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. @@ -1210,6 +1239,18 @@ def sync_do_groupby( The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) + + if with_counts: + out_with_counts = [] + + for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): + values_list = list(values) + out_with_counts.append( + _GroupTupleWithCount(key, values_list, len(values_list)) + ) + + return out_with_counts + return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) @@ -1222,11 +1263,25 @@ async def do_groupby( value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, + with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) + sorted_value = sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr) + + if with_counts: + out_with_counts = [] + + for key, values in groupby(sorted_value, expr): + values_list = await auto_to_list(values) + out_with_counts.append( + _GroupTupleWithCount(key, values_list, len(values_list)) + ) + + return out_with_counts + return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) - for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) + for key, values in groupby(sorted_value, expr) ]
